steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Identify the sampling method: Denise surveyed 24 families at a children's museum. The target population is all households in the community, but the sample was drawn only from families at a children's museum.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Assess representativeness: families found at a children's museum are systematically more likely to have children than the average household in the community. Families without children, or families whose children are grown, are excluded. This is a non-random, location-based sample — a textbook case of selection bias.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: Determine what "must be true": the sampling method is structurally flawed (not random), and it is likely to overestimate the mean number of children because it over-represents households with children. A biased estimate is the predictable consequence.

steps.title: Step 4
steps.content:
Evaluate each choice:
  • A: "The mean number of children per household in the community is 2.6" — we cannot conclude the population mean equals the sample mean from a biased sample. Eliminated.
  • B: "Should not be made because the sample size is too small" — sample size (24) is not the primary flaw here; the location bias is. This misstates the problem. Eliminated.
  • C: "The sampling method is flawed and may produce a biased estimate" — the children's-museum-only sample is non-random and creates selection bias. This must be true. C is correct.
  • D: "The sampling method is not flawed and is likely to produce an unbiased estimate" — directly contradicted by the location-based sampling. Eliminated.
